items.question: Explain what a 95% confidence interval means. What is a common misinterpretation?
items.answer: It means the interval comes from a procedure that, if repeated on many independent samples, would contain the true parameter about 95% of the time. The parameter is fixed; the interval is random. A common misinterpretation is claiming a 95% probability the true value lies in this particular interval after seeing the data—that is credible-interval language, not correct frequentist CI wording.

items.question: You have n = 25, x̄ = 40, s = 10. Sketch the 95% t-interval for the population mean.
items.answer: SE = 10/√25 = 2. t_{0.025, 24} ≈ 2.064. Margin = 2.064 × 2 ≈ 4.13. Interval ≈ [35.87, 44.13]. Use t because σ is unknown and n is modest.

items.question: How does a confidence interval relate to a two-sided hypothesis test at level α?
items.answer: For standard z/t/Wald constructions, a (1 − α) CI contains all parameter values that would not be rejected by a two-sided test at significance α. If θ₀ is outside the CI, reject H₀: θ = θ₀ at level α.

items.question: Why might you prefer a Wilson interval over a Wald interval for a conversion rate?
items.answer: Wald intervals use p̂ ± z√(p̂(1−p̂)/n), which can have poor coverage and produce bounds outside [0,1] when n is small or p̂ is near 0 or 1. Wilson intervals adjust toward 0.5 and typically achieve closer to nominal coverage.
